past_medical_history: ALS, DM, Frontal lobe dementia, Schizoaffective disorder
hpi_summary: The patient, a woman with ALS on a permanent ventilator, presented after becoming unresponsive during a difficult tracheostomy change at her rehab facility. She was found to have a fever, leukocytosis, and chest x-ray findings concerning for pneumonia. She was started on broad-spectrum antibiotics and transferred to the MICU for further care.
admission_vitals: Temperature: 100.8, Respiratory Rate: 20, SpO2: 95%, Temperature: 99, Heart Rate: 106, Blood Pressure: 96/61, Respiratory Rate: 22, SpO2: 97% on ventilator, General: Alert, no acute distress, nods head to answer questions.
discharge_vitals: Temperature: 97.8, Heart Rate: 67, Blood Pressure: 125/69, General: Alert, no acute distress, nods head to answer questions.
admission_labs: WBC: 17.1, Hemoglobin: 13.6, Creatinine: 0.3, Glucose: 207, Troponin: 0.04, Lactate: 1.1, Potassium: 5.3
microbiology_findings: Urine Culture: Escherichia coli (10,000-100,000 organisms/mL). Sensitive to Cefepime, Meropenem, Piperacillin/Tazobactam. Resistant to Ampicillin, Ceftriaxone, Ciprofloxacin.; Sputum Culture: Moderate growth of two morphologies of Pseudomonas aeruginosa with differing sensitivities. Also commensal respiratory flora.; Wound Culture (PEG site): Sparse growth of yeast.
hospital_course: The patient was managed for several issues. Ventilator-associated pneumonia due to Pseudomonas was treated with cefepime after developing acute interstitial nephritis likely from Zosyn, which resolved after discontinuation. She also had an E. coli UTI treated concurrently. The patient experienced an NSTEMI, managed medically with aspirin, statin, metoprolol, and a heparin drip. Her chronic conditions including ALS, DM, and nutritional needs were also managed.
antibiotic_plan: Continue cefepime for Pseudomonas VAP to complete course.
medications_started: Aspirin 81 mg daily, Atorvastatin 80 mg QPM, Metoprolol Tartrate 6.25 mg Q6H

discharge_medications: Docusate Sodium 100 mg PO daily, Ferrous Sulfate 300 mg PO daily, Glargine 10 Units with breakfast, Levetiracetam 750 mg PO BID, Quetiapine Fumarate 37.5 mg PO daily, Quetiapine Fumarate 75 mg PO QHS, Sertraline 150 mg PO daily, Aspirin 81 mg PO daily, Atorvastatin 80 mg PO QPM, Cefepime 1 g IV Q12H, Metoprolol Tartrate 6.25 mg PO Q6H, Glucerna oral daily, Guaifenesin PO Q4H, Lorazepam 0.5 mg PO Q8H, Omeprazole 40 mg PO daily, Quetiapine Fumarate 12.5 mg PO daily PRN agitation, Miconazole Powder 2% 1 application topically BID PRN irritation of PEG
primary_diagnoses: Ventilator Associated Pneumonia, Urinary tract infection, Non-ST elevation myocardial infarction, Acute interstitial nephritis
discharge_instructions: The patient was treated for lung and urinary tract infections with antibiotics, which will be continued for two weeks. She also had a minor heart attack due to the stress of infection and was started on new heart medications. She should take all medications as prescribed.

past_medical_history: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), Diabetes Mellitus (DM), Possible frontal lobe dementia, Possible schizoaffective disorder
hpi_summary: A ventilator-dependent female with ALS presented with decreased responsiveness, raising concern for non-convulsive status epilepticus. This followed a recent hospitalization for ventilator-associated pneumonia and NSTEMI. In the ED, she experienced episodes of unresponsiveness with eye deviation and hypotension, which were treated with lorazepam and a Keppra load before transfer to the MICU.
admission_vitals: Temperature: 96.8, Heart Rate: 98, Blood Pressure: 147/89, Respiratory Rate: 16, SpO2: 100%, General: Unresponsive
discharge_vitals: General: Awake, alert, tracheostomy in place
admission_labs: WBC: 13.0, Hemoglobin: 10.8, Platelets: 545, Creatinine: 0.6, Troponin: 0.16, Lactate: 1.1, BUN: 39, Sodium: 145, Potassium: 4.8
discharge_labs: WBC: 9.0, Hemoglobin: 8.8, Hematocrit: 27.3, Creatinine: 0.5, BUN: 13, Sodium: 139, Potassium: 4.0
microbiology_findings: Blood Culture: Pending; Urine Culture: Yeast; Sputum (Endotracheal): Gram stain showed >25 PMNs and gram-negative rods. Preliminary culture grew moderate amounts of three different gram-negative rods.; Bronchial Washings: Gram stain showed PMNs but no organisms. Preliminary culture grew gram-negative rods of three morphologies. Fungal culture was negative.
hospital_course: Altered mental status: Patient presented with unresponsiveness and eye deviation concerning for seizures. EEG showed no epileptiform activity, and episodes were later attributed to respiratory distress from mucous plugging. Antibiotics were changed from cefepime to ceftazidime due to seizure concern, and levetiracetam was continued.; ECG changes and troponin elevation: Patient had transient EKG changes and troponin elevation consistent with pericarditis, as seen on TTE. This was managed conservatively by continuing aspirin, statin, and metoprolol, as it was not thought to be an acute coronary event.; Ventilator-associated pneumonia: Continued treatment for VAP diagnosed during a prior admission. CXR showed persistent RLL infiltrate. She was afebrile with mild leukocytosis.; Acute hypoxic respiratory distress: Patient experienced an acute hypoxic event due to severe mucous plugging, primarily in the RLL, which was relieved by emergent bronchoscopy. This event caused transient hypertension and posturing, but was determined not to be seizure-related.; Chronic respiratory failure: Remained mechanically ventilated on her prior settings with an increase in PEEP from 5 to 8 to manage oxygenation.; Diabetes mellitus: Managed with home insulin regimen.; Hypertension: Metoprolol was started and titrated for blood pressure control.
antibiotic_plan: Continue ceftazidime for ventilator associated pneumonia until completion date.

primary_diagnoses: Altered mental status, Hypoxic respiratory distress
discharge_instructions: Patient was admitted for altered mental status, which was determined not to be seizures but likely related to a mucous plugging event. Cardiology confirmed no new heart attack. She will be discharged back to her facility to complete antibiotics for pneumonia and use a Cough Assist machine to prevent future mucous plugging.

past_medical_history: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), Diabetes Mellitus (DM), Possible frontal lobe dementia, Possible schizoaffective disorder
hpi_summary: A ventilator-dependent female with ALS was transferred for hypoxemia after an acute desaturation event at her facility, which improved with tracheal suctioning. She was initially treated for a possible pneumonia based on a preliminary chest x-ray, though the official read was clear. On arrival, she was calm and communicative via head nods.
admission_vitals: Temperature: 96.0, Heart Rate: 84, Blood Pressure: 124/80, Respiratory Rate: 20, SpO2: 98% on 40% FiO2, Temperature: 97.6, Heart Rate: 113, Blood Pressure: 103/41, Respiratory Rate: 22, SpO2: 100% on 40% FiO2, General: Alert, answers yes/no questions with nods, cannot verbalize
discharge_vitals: Heart Rate: 69, Blood Pressure: 124/76, Respiratory Rate: 14, SpO2: 99%, General: Alert trached female on ventilator, no acute distress
admission_labs: WBC: 11.8, Hemoglobin: 11.0, Creatinine: 0.5, Glucose: 171, Troponin: 0.16, Lactate: 1.9, BUN: 34, Potassium: 5.4
discharge_labs: WBC: 6.8, Hemoglobin: 8.5, Creatinine: 0.4, Glucose: 81, BUN: 13, Potassium: 3.6
microbiology_findings: Bronchial Washings: Gram stain showed PMNs but no organisms. Respiratory culture grew Acinetobacter baumannii complex, Stenotrophomonas maltophilia, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Pseudomonas was sensitive to Cefepime, Ceftazidime, Ciprofloxacin, Gentamicin, Meropenem, Piperacillin/Tazobactam, and Tobramycin. Fungal culture was negative.
hospital_course: Respiratory Failure: Hypoxemia was attributed to mucus plugging from ALS, which improved with suctioning and use of a cough assist device (MIE). Bronchoscopy was clear. G-tube placement: An improper Foley catheter in the G-tube stoma was replaced with an appropriate G-J tube. Troponinemia: Mildly elevated troponin was deemed non-cardiac and related to prior pericarditis or LVH. Seizure Prophylaxis: Continued on Keppra due to seizure risk. IDDM & Agitation: Diabetes and agitation were managed by continuing home insulin, Seroquel, and Sertraline.
